stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 attends her 6-week postnatal review. She delivered vaginally and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, no formula or solids. She has had no return of menstruation since delivery, and a urine beta-hCG is negative today. She and her partner do not wish to conceive again for at least 2 years. She has heard friends say 'breastfeeding stops periods so I don't need contraception' and asks whether that is true. She has no UKMEC category 3-4 contraindications.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 24, BP 118/74 mmHg. Uterus involuted. Breasts soft, no engorgement, infant feeding well.
data_table.Investigations: Urine beta-hCG negative. No further investigations performed today.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice and recommendation?

options.text: Counsel that LAM provides about 98% efficacy only up to 6 months post partum and only while she is amenorrhoeic and exclusively/near-exclusively breastfeeding; offer a parallel reliable method (LARC or POP) to avoid the gap when LAM expires
options.rationale: LAM is reliable only while ALL three criteria are met (exclusive/near-exclusive breastfeeding, infant under 6 months, amenorrhoea). At 6 weeks she meets criteria, but planning for the transition is part of good counselling: parallel LARC or progestogen-only methods bridge the gap (FSRH; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Reassure that breastfeeding alone provides reliable contraception for up to 12 months post partum
options.rationale: LAM reliable only up to 6 months, not 12; failure rate rises sharply once any criterion lapses. 12 months reliance is essentially never correct (FSRH).

options.text: Reassure that any breastfeeding plus amenorrhoea provides reliable contraception regardless of feeding pattern or solids
options.rationale: Partial or mixed feeding (with formula or solids) breaks LAM criteria; reliability drops markedly. Reassurance with mixed feeding is essentially never correct (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Counsel that LAM is reliable until 6 months only if one normal menstrual period has occurred to confirm cycles have restarted
options.rationale: Return of menstruation INVALIDATES LAM; criterion is amenorrhoea since delivery, not presence of menses. LAM-with-menses is essentially never correct (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Recommend commencing the combined oral contraceptive pill today as the most reliable bridging method
options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 in first 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months) due to VTE and supply concerns. COCP is correct >6 months postpartum or not breastfeeding (FSRH UKMEC).

explanation.summary: LAM provides about 98% contraceptive efficacy when all three criteria are met: exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding day and night, infant under 6 months, and amenorrhoea since delivery. Loss of any single criterion mandates an additional method.
key_points: Three LAM criteria: exclusive BF, <6 months, amenorrhoea
Progestogen-only pill can start from day 21 postpartum (any time if BF)
Combined hormonal contraception avoided until 6 weeks postpartum if BF (and 3 weeks if not BF, longer if VTE risk)
Postnatal IUD/IUS: usually inserted 4 to 6 weeks postpartum (immediate post-placental insertion also an option)
Etonogestrel implant: any time postpartum including day 0
explanation.pearls: If a woman wants LAM, document the three criteria explicitly and plan the bridging method for when any one fails first (usually the 6-month limit or weaning).

sources.label: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 woman presents to the emergency department on day 4 following an emergency lower segment caesarean section performed at 39+2 for failure to progress. She received cefazolin 2 g IV at skin incision intraoperatively. Over the last 24 hours she has developed fevers to 38.7 degC with rigors, lower abdominal pain, and increasingly offensive lochia which has changed from rubra to brown with a foul odour. She is breastfeeding with no breast engorgement, no breast erythema, no localised breast tenderness, no dysuria, no flank pain, no calf pain or swelling, and her caesarean wound looks clean and dry with no surrounding cellulitis. On examination her uterus is tender on bimanual palpation and is 4 cm above the symphysis. Bedside ultrasound shows an empty uterine cavity with no retained products. Cultures including blood, urine and high vaginal swab have been collected.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 108 bpm, T 38.7 degC, RR 20, SpO2 98%.
data_table.Examination: Lower abdomen tender, uterus tender at 4 cm above symphysis. Offensive lochia. Wound clean. Calves soft. Breasts soft. Renal angles non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 17.2 x10^9/L (neut 14.1), CRP 142 mg/L, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L. Urinalysis: nitrites neg, leuc neg. Pelvic USS: empty cavity, no RPOC.

lead_in: Which empirical intravenous antibiotic regimen is most appropriate according to current Australian Therapeutic Guidelines?

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily as monotherapy without any anaerobic or aminoglycoside cover today
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not reliably cover anaerobes or enterococci; not recommended for endometritis. Ceftriaxone alone is correct in less complicated post-partum infections without endometritis suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Flucloxacillin 2 g IV every 6 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg IV every 12 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin + metronidazole targets staphylococcal and anaerobic infection - inappropriate for polymicrobial gut/genital flora of endometritis; lacks adequate gram-negative cover. Flucloxa + metro is correct in selected wound infections, not endometritis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ampicillin 2 g IV every 6 hours plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily plus metronidazole 500 mg IV 12-hourly
options.rationale: Triple therapy with ampicillin (enterococci, streptococci), gentamicin (Gram-negative enteric bacilli) and metronidazole (anaerobes including Bacteroides) is the Therapeutic Guidelines empirical regimen for postpartum endometritis, reflecting its polymicrobial aetiology (Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RANZCOG).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 8 hours as broad-spectrum monotherapy without aminoglycoside or aerobic cover today
options.rationale: Pip-tazo offers broad polymicrobial cover but reserved for severe sepsis, HAI, or first-line failure. Pip-tazo is correct in severe sepsis or treatment failure (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily and discharge home today with GP follow-up tomorrow in clinic
options.rationale: Septic with HR 108, fever 38.7, WCC 17.2, CRP 142; IV antibiotics and inpatient management required. Outpatient oral is correct in mild endometritis without systemic features (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Postpartum endometritis is polymicrobial (Gram-positive cocci, Gram-negative bacilli, anaerobes including Bacteroides and Prevotella). The Australian Therapeutic Guidelines empirical regimen is IV ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole. Continue IV until afebrile for at least 24 to 48 hours, then complete a 10 to 14 day course if there is associated bacteraemia or slow response; otherwise oral step-down is not always required.
key_points: Risk factors: caesarean section (highest), prolonged ROM, chorioamnionitis, multiple VEs, manual placental removal
Diagnostic triad: fever, uterine tenderness, foul lochia (+/- elevated WCC and CRP)
Empirical: ampicillin 2 g q6h IV + gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily IV + metronidazole 500 mg q12h IV
Penicillin-allergic non-severe: substitute with cefazolin; severe allergy use clindamycin + gentamicin
Image with pelvic USS to exclude retained products and tubo-ovarian abscess if slow response
explanation.pearls: Endometritis is the single most common cause of postnatal fever between day 2 and day 10 after birth. Do not anchor on the wound; check lochia and bimanual uterine tenderness explicitly.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 exclusively breastfeeding her 3-week-old infant presents with a 36-hour history of a painful hot wedge of erythema in the upper outer right breast, fever 38.4, rigors, and infant feeding poorly from the affected side. Examination: 6 cm wedge erythema, warm, tender, NO fluctuance. Bedside USS shows no abscess. She has continued frequent feeding and warm compresses without improvement. Importantly, she has a documented IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity to penicillin (rash, urticaria and lip swelling 30 minutes after amoxicillin 5 years ago, confirmed by allergy clinic).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 38.4 degC.
data_table.Examination: Right breast upper outer quadrant: 6 cm wedge of erythema, warm and tender, no fluctuance, no overlying skin breakdown. Nipple intact. Left breast unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside USS (clinic): no discrete fluid collection. Milk culture not routinely performed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin is penicillin and contraindicated in IgE-mediated penicillin allergy. Flucloxacillin is correct in non-allergic women (eTG).

options.text: Cefalexin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
options.rationale: Cephalosporins carry ~1-2% cross-reactivity in immediate-type penicillin allergy; should be avoided in confirmed anaphylactic history. Cefalexin is correct in non-immediate penicillin allergy (eTG).

options.text: Clindamycin 450 mg orally every 8 hours for 5-10 days
options.rationale: For bacterial mastitis in a woman with immediate-type penicillin hypersensitivity, Therapeutic Guidelines recommend clindamycin as the first-line oral alternative; it is compatible with breastfeeding (eTG; ABM 2022).

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days
options.rationale: TMP-SMX contraindicated in infants <2 months (kernicterus risk); at 3 weeks best avoided. TMP-SMX is correct in older infants with MRSA mastitis (eTG).

options.text: Continue conservative measures alone for a further 48 hours
options.rationale: Clear bacterial mastitis (wedge erythema, fever 38.4, rigors) not responding to 36 hours of conservative care; antibiotics required. Conservative-only is correct in early inflammatory mastitis without systemic features (ABM 2022).

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis with systemic features (fever, increasing erythema, failure to improve after 24 hours of conservative care) is treated with oral flucloxacillin 500 mg every 6 hours for 5 to 10 days. Effective milk removal (continue breastfeeding or express) is essential. Ultrasound and aspiration are indicated if fluctuance or non-resolution suggests abscess.
key_points: S aureus is the predominant pathogen; MRSA cover only if local prevalence high or failed first-line
Continue breastfeeding from the affected breast - it accelerates recovery
Abscess: USS-guided aspiration is first-line in lactating women (avoid incision/drainage where possible to preserve future BF)
Inflammatory mastitis (no systemic features) often resolves with conservative care alone
Persistent unilateral mastitis not responding to 2 courses of antibiotics: exclude inflammatory breast cancer
explanation.pearls: Blocked duct = focal lump without systemic features. Inflammatory mastitis = redness + pain without abscess or systemic sepsis. Bacterial mastitis = above plus fever/tachycardia or non-resolution. Abscess = fluctuant collection on USS.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P3 woman has just had an emergency caesarean section at 38+5 weeks for foetal distress. Her pre-pregnancy BMI was 36 and her booking weight was 102 kg; she now weighs 108 kg on day 1 postpartum. She has a past history of one provoked deep vein thrombosis 8 years ago after a long-haul flight, was treated with warfarin for 6 months at that time, and has been off anticoagulation since. Her current pregnancy was uncomplicated apart from gestational diabetes managed with diet. She is a non-smoker, has no thrombophilia testing on record, no family history of unprovoked VTE, and her observations are stable. The estimated blood loss at caesarean was 600 mL with no further bleeding postoperatively. Her platelet count is 198 and her creatinine is 62 micromol/L. She is mobilising with help and is keen to breastfeed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Wound clean and dry. Calves soft, non-tender, symmetrical. Mobilising slowly with assistance.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 108 g/L, plt 198, creatinine 62 micromol/L, EBL at CS 600 mL. No active bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate postpartum thromboprophylaxis regimen?

options.text: Mechanical prophylaxis (TEDS plus early mobilisation) alone with no pharmacological prophylaxis
options.rationale: CS + BMI >30 + prior provoked VTE together exceed threshold for pharmacological prophylaxis on RANZCOG scoring; mechanical alone insufficient. Mechanical alone is correct in lower-risk patients (RANZCOG VTE).
source_refs: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Enoxaparin 20 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days as postpartum thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: 20 mg daily is standard for <50 kg or low-risk; this woman is 108 kg and high risk, requires weight-based dosing. 20 mg daily is correct in low-weight or low-risk women (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days only as postpartum prophylaxis
options.rationale: Weight-based dose (80 mg for 91-130 kg) appropriate, but duration of only 10 days inadequate given prior VTE history. 10-day duration is correct in low-risk women without prior VTE (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum

options.text: Enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously daily for 6 weeks as postpartum thromboprophylaxis
options.rationale: She has prior provoked VTE plus emergency caesarean plus BMI greater than 30 (a high-risk profile on RANZCOG/RCOG scoring). The recommended regimen is weight-banded LMWH (80 mg daily for 91 to 130 kg) for 6 weeks postpartum (RANZCOG; KEMH; RCOG GTG 37a).
source_refs: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum
RCOG GTG 37a Reducing the risk of VTE

options.text: Warfarin 5 mg daily for 6 weeks targeting INR 2.0 to 3.0 with bridging LMWH
options.rationale: Warfarin compatible with breastfeeding but LMWH preferred postpartum because no INR monitoring and avoids early postpartum warfarin instability. Warfarin is correct in long-term anticoagulation with stable INR (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE risk is stratified by RANZCOG/RCOG point scoring. Women with prior VTE almost always receive 6 weeks of LMWH at a weight-banded prophylactic dose; women with 4 or more transient risk factors receive 10 days; emergency caesarean alone with no other risk factors usually does not require pharmacological prophylaxis.
key_points: Weight-banded enoxaparin (typical bands): <50 kg 20 mg, 50-90 kg 40 mg, 91-130 kg 60-80 mg, 131-170 kg 80 mg twice daily considered
Duration: 10 days for transient risk; 6 weeks for prior VTE, persistent risk factors, high thrombophilia, or sustained admission
LMWH preferred over warfarin and DOACs postpartum (DOACs not used in breastfeeding women)
OHSS adds an independent high VTE risk that may warrant 6 weeks even without delivery risk factors
Hold LMWH if active bleeding or platelets <75 x10^9/L
explanation.pearls: On finals: any prior VTE = 6 weeks postpartum LMWH almost always. The decision is duration first, then dose by weight band.

sources.label: RANZCOG VTE in pregnancy and postpartum
RCOG GTG 37a Reducing the risk of VTE

subtopic_detail: Postpartum VTE thromboprophylaxis - RANZCOG risk-stratified dosing

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman attends a postnatal review at 5 weeks following an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4 weeks gestation. She is partially breastfeeding (approximately 60% breast milk, 40% formula because she returns to her finance role in 4 weeks and the workplace lacks lactation facilities) and has had no return of menses since delivery. Her booking BMI was 31, she is a lifelong non-smoker, her blood pressure today is 132/82 mmHg, and her booking and 36-week antenatal BP readings were both normotensive. She has no personal or family history of VTE or thrombophilia, no migraine with or without aura, no current or prior breast cancer, no hepatic disease, no diabetes, and no autoimmune disease. She and her partner want effective contraception starting as soon as possible today and. She is comfortable returning at any time for a procedure if that is the most appropriate option after counselling.

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 30, BP 132/82 mmHg. Uterus involuted. Breasts: lactating, no inflammation.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate contraceptive option to initiate today?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 microg/levonorgestrel 150 microg, starting today
options.rationale: CHC avoided in first 6 weeks postpartum breastfeeding (WHO MEC 3-4) due to theoretical effects on supply. COCP is correct >6 weeks postpartum if not breastfeeding (WHO MEC).
source_refs: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

options.text: Defer all contraception until 6 weeks postpartum and rely on LAM in the interim
options.rationale: Does not meet LAM criteria because only partially breastfeeding; deferral risks ovulation. LAM is correct only with exclusive breastfeeding + amenorrhoea + <6 months postpartum (FSRH).

options.text: Insertion of a copper intrauterine device at 5 weeks postpartum after speculum examination
options.rationale: Cu-IUD insertion within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks postpartum, so feasible at 5 weeks; however requires procedure scheduling. Cu-IUD is correct option when willing to wait for insertion appointment (WHO MEC).

options.text: Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system today at 5 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: LNG-IUS insertion follows similar timing; requires procedure scheduling and speculum work. LNG-IUS is correct option when willing to wait for insertion (WHO MEC).

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant inserted today during the consultation
options.rationale: The etonogestrel implant is WHO MEC category 1 at any time postpartum in breastfeeding women, can be inserted during the postnatal consultation, has the highest typical-use efficacy of any reversible method, and avoids the VTE and lactation concerns of combined hormonal contraception in the early postpartum window (WHO MEC; FSRH; Family Planning Australia).
source_refs: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

explanation.summary: Postnatal contraception timing: progestogen-only methods (POP, implant) can be started any time postpartum including day 0. Cu-IUD and LNG-IUS are inserted within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks. Combined hormonal contraception is generally avoided until 6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding (and until 3 weeks if not breastfeeding, longer if VTE risk factors).
key_points: Implant: any time, WHO MEC 1 in BF women
POP: from day 21 (any time in BF women)
Cu-IUD / LNG-IUS: <48 h or >=4 weeks postpartum
COCP: avoid <3 weeks postpartum (VTE), <6 weeks if BF
LAM only reliable with exclusive BF + <6 months + amenorrhoea
explanation.pearls: Best practice is to discuss postnatal contraception antenatally and offer the implant at hospital discharge or within the first 5 days to capture women before contact is lost.

sources.label: WHO Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use

subtopic_detail: Postnatal contraception - timing of method initiation in a partially breastfeeding woman

stem: A 30-year-old G1P1 woman attends her general practice for a 6-week postnatal review having delivered a healthy term infant by an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth. She describes 3 weeks of persistent low mood, anhedonia, tearfulness on most days, poor sleep that is markedly worse than would be expected from infant feeding alone, poor concentration, reduced appetite, and pervasive feelings of worthlessness and guilt as a mother. She denies thoughts of self-harm, denies active suicidal ideation, denies thoughts of harming the infant, and denies hallucinations or unusual beliefs that would raise concern for postpartum psychosis. She has no past psychiatric history other than mild generalised anxiety in late adolescence that resolved without treatment, no family history of bipolar affective disorder, and no current substance use. She is exclusively breastfeeding her infant, has a supportive partner who has taken parental leave, and has no domestic violence concerns. Her Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale completed today is 16, with the self-harm item (item 10) scored zero. Thyroid function tests sent last week were normal (TSH 1.4 mIU/L, free T4 14 pmol/L).

data_table.Examination: Well, BMI 24, BP 116/72 mmHg. Affect flat. Insight preserved. No psychotic features. No suicidal ideation.
data_table.Investigations: EPDS 16 (self-harm item 0). TSH 1.4 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L. Hb 122 g/L, ferritin 48 micrograms/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line antidepressant to commence?

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has longest SSRI half-life and long-lived active metabolite (norfluoxetine), leading to higher relative infant dose. Fluoxetine is correct when previously responsive or sertraline not tolerated (LactMed).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrated up as needed
options.rationale: Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women in Australian and international perinatal guidelines because of low relative infant dose, short half-life and the largest evidence base demonstrating safety in lactation (COPE; AMH; LactMed).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

options.text: Paroxetine 20 mg orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Paroxetine has acceptable breastfeeding data but associated with discontinuation syndromes; preferred when previously successful. Paroxetine is correct in patients with prior paroxetine response (COPE).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg modified-release orally daily as antidepressant
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is SNRI not first-line for new-onset moderate postnatal depression; reasonable second-line after SSRI failure. Venlafaxine is correct after SSRI failure (COPE).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023

options.text: Lithium carbonate 500 mg orally at night as monotherapy
options.rationale: Lithium generally avoided in breastfeeding due to high relative infant dose and infant toxicity risk (tremor, dehydration, thyroid); reserved for severe bipolar requiring lithium-based maintenance. Lithium is correct in bipolar with specialist input only (LactMed).
source_refs: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

explanation.summary: EPDS at or above 13 prompts further assessment; combined with a 3-week history of pervasive low mood and anhedonia, this woman meets criteria for moderate postnatal depression. Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in breastfeeding women per COPE and AMH because of low transfer into milk and the strongest safety evidence. Continue psychological therapy concurrently.
key_points: EPDS cutoff: >=13 prompts further assessment; specific item 10 screens self-harm
Differential: postpartum blues (peaks day 3 to 5, resolves by day 14), PND (any time in first year), postpartum psychosis (urgent, often early)
Sertraline first-line SSRI in lactation; paroxetine alternative
Avoid fluoxetine first-line in BF; avoid lithium unless bipolar
Always screen for postpartum psychosis: confusion, hallucinations, mania, infant harm thoughts - admit urgently
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the answer in essentially every Australian medical exam scenario asking for an SSRI in lactation. Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency requiring urgent specialist admission, not outpatient SSRI.

sources.label: COPE Mental Health Care in the Perinatal Period 2023
AMH 2024 - SSRIs in pregnancy and lactation

subtopic_detail: Postnatal depression - first-line SSRI in a breastfeeding woman

stem: A 26-year-old G1P1 woman has just delivered a 4.1 kg infant by spontaneous vaginal birth with a 30-minute active second stage. The delivery was attended by a midwife with an obstetric registrar called for shoulder dystocia which was resolved with McRoberts and suprapubic pressure within 90 seconds. There was a head-to-body interval of 80 seconds. After delivery of the placenta, careful inspection of the perineum demonstrates a tear extending through the perineal skin, perineal muscles, and partial-thickness laceration of the external anal sphincter involving approximately 40% of its thickness; the internal anal sphincter and rectal mucosa appear intact on careful per rectum examination. The registrar grades the injury as 3a. Maternal observations are stable, the infant is in good condition with Apgars 9 and 9, and the estimated blood loss is 350 mL with no ongoing bleeding. The hospital has a theatre available now and a senior obstetrician on site.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Grade 3a tear: <50% external anal sphincter thickness, IAS and rectal mucosa intact. EBL 350 mL.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L. Group and screen on file.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management approach for repair and after-care?

options.text: Repair on the labour ward bed under local anaesthesia using 2-0 polyglactin (Vicryl) for all layers, no postoperative antibiotics, encourage normal diet, no physiotherapy referral
options.rationale: On-ward local-anaesthetic repair with no antibiotics or follow-up is wrong because OASI requires theatre repair under regional/GA with adequate lighting and exposure; broad-spectrum antibiotics, laxatives and structured follow-up (physiotherapy, perineal clinic) are mandatory. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

options.text: Repair on the ward without anaesthesia, give one course of oral metronidazole, no laxative and no perineal clinic follow-up
options.rationale: Ward repair without anaesthesia is wrong because OASI repair requires theatre-level anaesthesia for adequate visualisation and sphincter alignment; on-ward repair without anaesthesia compromises outcome. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

options.text: Repair in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics intraoperatively then oral course, lactulose for 10 days, pelvic floor physiotherapy referral, perineal clinic follow-up
options.rationale: OASI requires repair in theatre with adequate anaesthesia, regional or GA, broad-spectrum antibiotic cover, stool softeners (typically lactulose) for at least 10 days, structured pelvic floor physiotherapy referral, and follow-up in a specialist perineal clinic at 6 to 12 weeks with assessment for symptoms of incontinence (RCOG GTG 29; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

options.text: Repair in theatre, oral cephalexin only, no stool softener (constipation will protect the repair), no physiotherapy referral, GP review at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Theatre repair but no laxative is wrong because constipation increases dehiscence and pain; lactulose for at least 10 days is standard. Narrow staphylococcal cover alone is also inadequate - broad-spectrum cover is needed. Physiotherapy and perineal clinic are part of structured follow-up. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

options.text: Defer repair for 24 hours to allow tissue oedema to settle, then repair in theatre, no antibiotics, lactulose for 3 days, physiotherapy referral
options.rationale: Defer repair 24h is wrong because OASI should be repaired as soon as feasible by a trained operator; delay increases infection risk. Antibiotic prophylaxis is required and laxative for at least 10 days. Would never be correct (RCOG GTG 29).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

explanation.summary: OASI (grade 3a, 3b, 3c, 4) repair: theatre with regional or general anaesthesia, end-to-end or overlap repair of the external anal sphincter with 2-0 or 3-0 PDS or polyglactin, separate IAS repair if torn, broad-spectrum IV then oral antibiotics, stool softeners for at least 10 days, and structured pelvic floor physiotherapy plus perineal clinic follow-up at 6 to 12 weeks. Counsel about mode of next delivery once symptomatic status is known.
key_points: Grades: 3a <50% EAS, 3b >50% EAS, 3c IAS torn, 4 anal mucosa torn
Repair in theatre with adequate lighting and assistance
Suture: 2-0 or 3-0 PDS for sphincters, 2-0 polyglactin for vaginal mucosa/perineal muscles
Antibiotics: cefuroxime + metronidazole IV then oral course (Therapeutic Guidelines analogous regimen)
Lactulose 10 days; physiotherapy referral; perineal clinic 6-12 weeks
explanation.pearls: Risk factors for OASI: primiparity, instrumental delivery (forceps > vacuum), prolonged second stage, macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, persistent OP. Mediolateral episiotomy reduces OASI risk in instrumental delivery.

sources.label: RCOG GTG 29 Third- and fourth-degree perineal tears

subtopic_detail: Obstetric anal sphincter injury repair - principles of management

stem: A 34-year-old G2P2 woman attends her general practitioner 5 months after the delivery of her second child. The pregnancy itself was uncomplicated until delivery, when she had a massive postpartum haemorrhage estimated at 3500 mL secondary to uterine atony plus a small lower segment extension during an emergency caesarean section for foetal distress; she required 6 units of packed red cells, 2 units of fresh frozen plasma, 1 unit of cryoprecipitate, intraoperative tranexamic acid, intramyometrial carboprost, a Bakri balloon, and spent 36 hours in the high-dependency unit before stabilising. Since then she has been unable to establish breastfeeding despite repeated attempts in the first week with a lactation consultant, has had no return of menses at all over the last 5 months, has lost approximately 4 kg of muscle mass with reduced exercise tolerance, sleeps 11 hours daily and is still fatigued during the day, feels persistently cold even in warm rooms, has noticed loss of axillary and pubic hair, has had two episodes of presyncope on standing in the last month, and has reduced libido. On examination today she is thin, pale, has dry skin, fine sparse hair, and a postural blood pressure drop from 108/68 mmHg supine to 86/52 mmHg standing. Her thyroid gland is non-tender and non-enlarged on palpation, and there is no goitre.

data_table.Vital signs: BP supine 108/68 mmHg, standing 86/52 mmHg. HR 58 bpm, T 36.2 degC.
data_table.Examination: Thin, pale, dry skin, fine sparse axillary and pubic hair. Thyroid non-palpable. No goitre.
data_table.Investigations: Pending - GP is determining the most appropriate first-line endocrine workup.

lead_in: Which combination of investigations is most appropriate to establish the diagnosis of suspected Sheehan syndrome?

options.text: Morning 0800 cortisol, TSH plus free T4, LH, FSH, oestradiol, prolactin, IGF-1, and MRI pituitary
options.rationale: Sheehan syndrome causes panhypopituitarism after pituitary ischaemic necrosis following major PPH. The investigation strategy is to assess each pituitary axis: cortisol (ACTH axis), free T4 with TSH (TSH axis - TSH may be inappropriately normal or low), LH/FSH and oestradiol (gonadotrophin axis), prolactin (lactotroph axis - typically low or undetectable), IGF-1 (GH axis), with MRI demonstrating an empty or partially empty sella (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: TSH and free T4 only, treating empirically with levothyroxine if either is low
options.rationale: TSH/T4 only with empirical thyroxine is wrong because TSH/T4 alone miss the potentially fatal cortisol deficiency; giving thyroxine before replacing glucocorticoid can precipitate adrenal crisis. Would be correct only after cortisol replacement is confirmed adequate (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Random cortisol and random TSH on the day of consultation only as initial work-up
options.rationale: Random cortisol and TSH is wrong because random cortisol is unreliable due to diurnal variation; 0800 morning cortisol plus the full pituitary axis screen plus imaging is required. Would never be correct as a complete workup (Endocrine Society).

options.text: 24-hour urinary free cortisol and an overnight dexamethasone suppression test as the first step
options.rationale: 24h urinary cortisol and dexamethasone suppression is wrong because these tests screen for Cushing syndrome (excess cortisol), not deficiency; the workup is in the wrong direction entirely. Would be correct only when suspecting Cushing (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound and anti-Mullerian hormone level to assess her ovarian reserve as first step
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound and AMH is wrong because amenorrhoea in Sheehan is central (low LH/FSH and oestradiol), not primary ovarian failure; AMH would not establish the pituitary diagnosis. Would be correct only for ovarian reserve assessment, not pituitary workup (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Sheehan syndrome is ischaemic necrosis of the anterior pituitary following severe postpartum haemorrhage. Failure to lactate (loss of prolactin) is often the earliest feature; secondary amenorrhoea, cold intolerance, fatigue, hyponatraemia and postural hypotension follow. Workup screens each pituitary axis and includes MRI. Treat with glucocorticoid replacement first (hydrocortisone), then thyroxine, then sex steroid replacement; GH replacement is considered in symptomatic adults.
key_points: Failure to lactate is the most specific early clue
Treat cortisol before thyroxine to avoid adrenal crisis
Hyponatraemia is common (cortisol deficiency, hypothyroidism)
MRI may show empty or partially empty sella
Prolactin is typically low or undetectable (versus high in pituitary adenoma)
explanation.pearls: Any postpartum woman with prior major PPH who fails to lactate, fails to menstruate, and presents with non-specific fatigue, hypotension or cold intolerance should have a pituitary workup until proven otherwise. Hydrocortisone before levothyroxine - always.

subtopic_detail: Sheehan syndrome - investigation strategy after severe postpartum haemorrhage

stem: A 28-year-old G2P2 on day 2 post-vaginal-birth (PPH 1100 mL controlled with uterotonics and tranexamic acid; no transfusion) feels tired but not breathless, ambulates without symptoms, breastfeeds well. Booking Hb 124 g/L. Today: Hb 78 g/L, MCV 76, ferritin 12 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 9%, platelets 240, normal renal function. No active bleeding, no chest pain, no syncope. She wishes to be discharged tomorrow to care for her toddler. Local protocol follows the National Blood Authority Patient Blood Management Module 5 (Obstetrics).

data_table.Vital signs: BP 112/72 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae. Lungs clear. Cardiac exam unremarkable. Mobilising independently. No active bleeding.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 12 micrograms/L, transferrin sat 9%, plts 240. EBL 1100 mL at delivery.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of her postpartum iron deficiency anaemia?

options.text: Single dose IV ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg before discharge with review at 4 weeks
options.rationale: Hb 70-80 g/L in a stable postpartum woman with no ongoing bleeding does not mandate transfusion (PBM Module 5: transfusion thresholds Hb <70 g/L in stable patients). IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) corrects iron stores rapidly, supports breastfeeding and avoids transfusion risks (NBA PBM5; AMH).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells before discharge
options.rationale: Transfuse 2 units is wrong because she is asymptomatic at rest with stable haemodynamics and no ongoing bleeding; the NBA PBM5 restrictive threshold is Hb <70 g/L in stable patients. Would be correct only with symptomatic anaemia, ongoing bleeding or Hb <70 (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

options.text: Oral ferrous sulphate 325 mg three times daily for 3 months
options.rationale: Oral ferrous sulphate TDS for 3 months is wrong because at this Hb level oral iron corrects slowly (months) and is poorly tolerated (GI side effects); IV iron is preferred for postpartum severe deficiency. Would be correct only for mild postpartum anaemia with good tolerance (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

options.text: Oral ferrous fumarate 200 mg three times daily with no scheduled follow-up
options.rationale: Oral ferrous fumarate TDS no follow-up is wrong because oral iron alone for severe deficiency is suboptimal and follow-up is required to confirm correction. Would never be correct (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

options.text: Erythropoietin-stimulating agent subcutaneously now plus oral iron for 3 months
options.rationale: ESA plus oral iron is wrong because ESAs are not routinely indicated in postpartum iron deficiency without renal disease; IV iron alone suffices. Would be correct only with renal-disease-associated anaemia not responding to iron (NBA PBM5; AMH).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

explanation.summary: Australian Patient Blood Management Guidelines recommend: transfuse if Hb less than 70 g/L or symptomatic; IV iron if Hb 70 to 90 g/L with iron deficiency or oral iron intolerance or need for rapid response; oral iron for Hb at or above 90 g/L without urgency. Ferritin less than 30 micrograms/L confirms iron deficiency in the postpartum population.
key_points: Transfusion threshold postpartum: Hb <70 g/L or symptomatic compromise
IV iron indications: Hb 70-90 g/L with ID; oral intolerance; need for rapid response; ongoing absorption issues
Ferritin <30 micrograms/L = iron deficiency (consider <100 if inflammation)
Ferric carboxymaltose: 1000 mg single dose, infused over 15 minutes
Oral iron: alternate-day dosing improves absorption; review Hb at 4 weeks
explanation.pearls: In the postpartum setting, IV iron is increasingly the workhorse for moderate anaemia because it is faster, more reliable and avoids the constipation that compounds perineal pain and post-caesarean recovery.

sources.label: Australian Patient Blood Management Guidelines - Module 5 Obstetrics and Maternity

subtopic_detail: Postpartum iron deficiency anaemia - transfusion vs IV iron vs oral iron thresholds

stem: A 24-year-old G1P1 woman is reviewed 7 hours after a forceps-assisted vaginal birth at 40+1 with a mediolateral episiotomy. She had an epidural sited in labour and her last top-up was 5 hours ago; sensation in her legs has now fully returned. The estimated blood loss was 450 mL and her perineal tear is a grade 2 episiotomy repair with no extension into the sphincter complex. She last passed urine spontaneously 9 hours before the start of pushing, which was 11 hours ago in total. She has been encouraged to attempt voiding three times over the last 4 hours but each attempt has produced less than 50 mL despite a sensation of fullness. Bladder ultrasound at the bedside shows 720 mL of urine in the bladder. She has had no haematuria, no flank pain, no abdominal distension causing pain, and her observations are stable. She is keen to mobilise to the shower and start skin-to-skin.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Suprapubic fullness, non-tender. Episiotomy repair intact. Sensation in legs fully returned. Pad: minimal lochia.
data_table.Investigations: Bedside bladder USS: 720 mL. Urinalysis pending.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Encourage further oral fluids now and then re-scan her bladder again in 4 hours
options.rationale: Encourage more fluids and re-scan in 4 hours is wrong because 720 mL after 11 hours of failed voiding meets retention criteria requiring drainage; continued conservative measures risk detrusor overdistension and long-term voiding dysfunction. Would be correct only with smaller residual volume and short time since last void (KEMH; RCOG).

options.text: Insert in-out catheter, drain bladder once, then discharge to postnatal ward without further plan
options.rationale: Single in-out catheter then discharge is wrong because single drainage without a structured plan risks recurrent retention; indwelling catheter for 24-48h plus structured TWOC is needed when residual >500-700 mL. Would be correct only with residual <500 mL and good spontaneous voiding (KEMH).

options.text: Refer her urgently to urology for cystoscopy under general anaesthesia today in theatre setting
options.rationale: Urgent urology cystoscopy is wrong because postpartum urinary retention is typically transient and managed by obstetric teams with catheterisation and TWOC; urology referral is not first-line. Would be correct only with persistent retention after multiple failed TWOCs or suspected structural pathology (RCOG).

options.text: Administer IV furosemide 20 mg and then re-scan her bladder again in 1 hour
options.rationale: IV furosemide is wrong because furosemide treats fluid overload not retention; it would worsen the problem by increasing bladder volume further. Would never be correct in retention (KEMH).

options.text: Insert indwelling urinary catheter on free drainage for 24-48 hours, then trial of void with bladder scans
options.rationale: Bladder volume greater than 500 to 700 mL with failed voiding after vaginal birth defines covert/overt postpartum urinary retention. Management is indwelling catheterisation for 24 to 48 hours to allow detrusor recovery, followed by a structured trial of void with pre- and post-void scans; persistent residual volumes greater than 150 mL prompt repeat catheterisation and further investigation (KEMH; RCOG; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Postpartum urinary retention is defined as failure to void within 6 hours of vaginal birth or post-void residual greater than 150 mL after attempted void. Risk factors include epidural, prolonged second stage, instrumental delivery, OASI, episiotomy, and large infant. Management is indwelling catheter for 24 to 48 hours then trial of void with bladder scans.
key_points: Catheterise within 6 hours of vaginal birth if no spontaneous void
Bladder volume >500-700 mL: indwelling catheter 24-48 h preferred over single in-out
TWOC after 24-48 h with pre- and post-void scans
PVR >150 mL: re-catheterise and reassess
Risk factors: epidural, instrumental, prolonged labour, OASI, big baby
explanation.pearls: Detrusor overdistension can cause permanent voiding dysfunction. Document time and volume of every void in the first 24 hours postpartum, and act on the first failed attempt rather than waiting.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum urinary retention - identification and management

stem: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman was diagnosed with preeclampsia at 36 weeks gestation and delivered at 37+0 by induction of labour with a spontaneous vaginal birth, complicated only by a small first-degree perineal tear that was sutured at the time. Antenatally she received labetalol 200 mg three times daily plus methyldopa 250 mg three times daily for blood pressure control, achieving target BP below 140/90 mmHg from 34 weeks. She is now day 6 postpartum and is breastfeeding her infant exclusively with good supply. Her blood pressure today on home monitoring has been 158/102 mmHg, 154/96 mmHg and 156/100 mmHg recorded over the last 24 hours despite continuing both of her antenatal antihypertensives at full antenatal dose. She has no headache, no visual disturbance, no epigastric pain, no nausea, no oliguria, no shortness of breath, and no peripheral oedema beyond mild ankle swelling. Her bloods this morning show a normal full blood count without thrombocytopaenia, normal renal function with creatinine 70 micromol/L, AST and ALT within normal limits, and a urine protein-creatinine ratio elevated at 60 mg/mmol (down from a peak of 180 mg/mmol antenatally).

data_table.Vital signs: BP today average 156/99 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: No focal neurology. No epigastric tenderness. Mild ankle oedema. Breasts soft.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, plts 220, creatinine 70 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L, urine PCR 60 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate adjustment to her antihypertensive regimen?

options.text: Increase methyldopa to 500 mg three times daily and continue current labetalol dose
options.rationale: Increase methyldopa is wrong because methyldopa is associated with postpartum depression and sedation and is typically weaned in the puerperium rather than uptitrated; SOMANZ prefers other agents postpartum. Would never be correct as escalation strategy (SOMANZ).

options.text: Wean and stop methyldopa, increase labetalol, add nifedipine controlled-release 30 mg daily if BP elevated
options.rationale: SOMANZ recommends weaning methyldopa postpartum (depression risk, no clear postpartum benefit), continuing or uptitrating labetalol and adding a calcium channel blocker such as nifedipine controlled-release as a second agent; all three are compatible with breastfeeding (SOMANZ; AMH).

options.text: Switch labetalol to atenolol 50 mg daily and continue current methyldopa dose
options.rationale: Atenolol is wrong because atenolol concentrates in breast milk and can cause neonatal bradycardia, hypotension and hypoglycaemia; it is avoided in breastfeeding. Would be correct only outside breastfeeding (AMH).

options.text: Commence oral enalapril 5 mg daily and stop labetalol now in this patient
options.rationale: Enalapril replacing labetalol immediately is wrong because while enalapril is breastfeeding-compatible, SOMANZ prefers stepwise change with labetalol or nifedipine as first-line postpartum and methyldopa weaning pending. Would be correct only after stepwise titration of preferred agents (SOMANZ).

options.text: Stop all antihypertensives now because she is more than 5 days postpartum currently
options.rationale: Stop all antihypertensives at >5 days postpartum is wrong because BP characteristically peaks at days 3-6 postpartum and pre-eclampsia can present or worsen up to 6 weeks postpartum; treatment continues while BP remains >=150/100. Would be correct only with sustained BP normalisation (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Postpartum hypertension management after preeclampsia: continue and adjust antihypertensives, monitor BP for at least 6 weeks postpartum, wean methyldopa (depression risk), and use labetalol, nifedipine, enalapril or captopril as preferred agents in breastfeeding. Preeclampsia can present de novo up to 6 weeks postpartum.
key_points: Wean methyldopa postpartum (depression risk)
Labetalol, nifedipine CR, enalapril, captopril all compatible with BF
Avoid atenolol in BF (neonatal beta-blockade)
Treat BP >=150/100 mmHg sustained; aim BP <140/90 mmHg postpartum
Monitor BP and proteinuria up to 6 weeks; postpartum review at 6 weeks plus 6 to 12 months for CVD risk
explanation.pearls: Postnatal new-onset headache plus elevated BP within 6 weeks of delivery is preeclampsia or eclampsia until proven otherwise. Always screen mood in postpartum hypertensive women - methyldopa is a hidden contributor.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum hypertension - antihypertensive choice in a breastfeeding woman with prior preeclampsia

stem: A 36-year-old G1P1 woman attends her general practitioner at 10 days postpartum because, despite frequent on-demand feeding and continuous skin-to-skin contact since birth, she has produced very little breast milk and her infant has lost 11% of birth weight on weighed feeds today. The delivery was at 38+5 by elective lower segment caesarean section for breech presentation; the third stage was complicated by a placenta with a retained cotyledon noted on inspection that was manually removed in theatre under spinal anaesthesia, with no postpartum haemorrhage (estimated blood loss 380 mL total). She had no antenatal complications, normal booking and 36-week bloods including a normal FBC and ferritin, and was discharged on day 4 in stable condition. She noticed no antenatal breast enlargement during pregnancy, which was unusual compared with her friends. On examination her breasts are small, tubular, widely spaced and noticeably asymmetric (left larger than right), with minimal glandular fullness on palpation and no breast engorgement; there are no signs of inflammation, infection, fluctuance, or mastitis. Her uterus is involuted appropriately to size, her perineum is healed, and her observations are all stable. She is not on any medications, has no past breast surgery, no nipple piercings, and has been seen by an IBCLC lactation consultant twice with optimal latch, optimal positioning, and a normal infant suck assessment confirmed.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 80 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Breasts small, asymmetric, widely spaced, minimal glandular tissue, no inflammation. Uterus involuted. Infant: well, tone good, suck normal on assessment.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation to clarify the cause of failure to establish lactation?

options.text: Infant tongue-tie clinical assessment by paediatric ENT for surgical release if confirmed
options.rationale: Infant tongue-tie assessment is wrong because optimal latch and positioning and a normal infant suck have already been confirmed by lactation consultant assessment; a primary infant-side cause is unlikely. Would be correct only with suspected suck problems (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Maternal full blood count and ferritin level only as initial work-up
options.rationale: Maternal FBC and ferritin only is wrong because anaemia may contribute to fatigue but rarely causes complete failure of lactogenesis II; targeted prolactin and pituitary axis testing is needed. Would be correct only as adjunct to the targeted workup (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Pelvic ultrasound only as the initial single investigation for her presentation today
options.rationale: Pelvic ultrasound only is wrong because while pelvic USS helps exclude retained products, it is most useful in combination with prolactin and wider pituitary workup; alone it misses pituitary, glandular and endocrine causes. Would be correct only as part of comprehensive workup (ABA).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Serum prolactin and pituitary axis screen (cortisol, TSH and free T4, LH and FSH)
options.rationale: Failure to establish lactation despite optimal latch and positioning prompts investigation for hypoprolactinaemia (Sheehan), retained products causing persistent progesterone elevation, primary insufficient glandular tissue (suggested here by tubular, widely spaced, asymmetric breasts) and significant stress. Serum prolactin is the targeted first investigation, with concurrent screening of the pituitary axis to exclude Sheehan especially in any woman with peripartum bleeding or retained tissue (ABA; LactMed; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

options.text: Empirical trial of oral domperidone 10 mg three times daily without further investigation today
options.rationale: Empirical domperidone without investigation is wrong because galactagogues should follow characterisation of the underlying cause; empirical treatment risks missing Sheehan and retained products which need specific management. Would be correct only after underlying cause is established and primary low supply is confirmed (ABA; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

explanation.summary: Failure to establish lactation has several distinct causes: delayed lactogenesis II (most common, transient), retained placental fragments (progesterone continues to inhibit prolactin), Sheehan syndrome (low prolactin after major PPH), insufficient glandular tissue (tubular, asymmetric, widely spaced breasts), prior breast surgery, severe stress, and PCOS. Investigation is targeted: prolactin and pituitary axis first, plus pelvic USS for retained products if any third-stage concern.
key_points: Lactogenesis II is the copious milk production phase that normally begins at day 2 to 5 postpartum
Retained placental tissue continues progesterone production and inhibits lactogenesis II
IGT clinical features: tubular, widely spaced, asymmetric breasts; minimal antenatal growth
Sheehan presents with failure to lactate as an early sign
Galactagogues (domperidone, metoclopramide) only after diagnostic workup
explanation.pearls: If you cannot tick off latch, positioning, frequency, and infant transfer as adequate, fix those first. If they are adequate and supply remains poor, investigate the mother.

sources.label: Australian Breastfeeding Association - Low milk supply

subtopic_detail: Failure to lactate - differential and investigation

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her general practitioner 4 months postpartum with a 6-week history of palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety and a 3 kg weight loss despite an increased appetite. She is breastfeeding her infant exclusively. She has no neck pain, no recent viral illness, no goitre on examination, no eye signs, no family history of thyroid disease, and no past psychiatric history. Her pregnancy was uncomplicated, the delivery was a spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4, and she had no obstetric or postpartum complications. Her bloods today show TSH less than 0.01 mIU/L, free T4 32 pmol/L, free T3 9.2 pmol/L, anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies strongly positive at 480 IU/mL, and thyroid receptor (TRAb) antibodies negative. A radioiodine uptake scan would be contraindicated because of breastfeeding but a thyroid ultrasound shows a diffusely heterogeneous gland with reduced vascularity on Doppler.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 108 bpm regular, T 36.9 degC.
data_table.Examination: Fine tremor, warm peripheries, no goitre, no eye signs. Breastfeeding well.
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01 mIU/L, fT4 32 pmol/L, fT3 9.2 pmol/L, anti-TPO 480 IU/mL (strongly +), TRAb negative. USS thyroid: diffusely heterogeneous, reduced vascularity.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of her current thyrotoxic phase?

options.text: Carbimazole 30 mg orally daily, titrated to repeat thyroid function tests every 4 weeks
options.rationale: Carbimazole is wrong because postpartum thyroiditis causes thyrotoxicosis by destructive release of preformed hormone, not new synthesis; antithyroid drugs (carbimazole, PTU) are ineffective. Would be correct only in Graves or other synthesis-mediated thyrotoxicosis (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy 2017

options.text: Radioiodine ablation as soon as breastfeeding can be temporarily ceased entirely
options.rationale: Radioiodine ablation is wrong because radioiodine is for Graves disease or toxic nodular disease, not transient destructive thyroiditis; postpartum thyroiditis typically resolves spontaneously. Would never be correct here (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy 2017

options.text: Propranolol 10-40 mg three times daily for symptom control, monitoring thyroid function every 6-8 weeks
options.rationale: The thyrotoxic phase of postpartum thyroiditis is destructive (reduced vascularity, anti-TPO positive, TRAb negative) and treated symptomatically with a beta-blocker such as propranolol while monitoring TFTs every 6 to 8 weeks. Spontaneous resolution into euthyroidism or progression to a hypothyroid phase typically follows; permanent hypothyroidism occurs in approximately 20% at 1 year (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy 2017

options.text: Levothyroxine 50 microg orally daily and review her thyroid function tests in 8 weeks
options.rationale: Levothyroxine is wrong because she is currently thyrotoxic (TSH <0.01, fT4 32); levothyroxine would worsen her state. Would be correct only in the subsequent hypothyroid phase or permanent hypothyroidism (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy 2017

options.text: Lugol iodine and oral dexamethasone for treatment of thyroid storm now
options.rationale: Lugol iodine plus dexamethasone for thyroid storm is wrong because thyroid storm requires severe features (high fever, marked tachycardia, altered consciousness, organ dysfunction) which are absent; her thyrotoxicosis is mild-to-moderate. Would be correct only with established thyroid storm (BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: Postpartum thyroiditis is a destructive lymphocytic thyroiditis presenting 1 to 12 months postpartum, classically biphasic: thyrotoxic phase (1 to 4 months postpartum, lasting weeks to months) followed by a hypothyroid phase (4 to 8 months postpartum, also transient). Most resolve to euthyroidism; approximately 20% develop permanent hypothyroidism. Anti-TPO antibodies are usually positive. Treat the thyrotoxic phase with a beta-blocker only, and the hypothyroid phase with levothyroxine if symptomatic.
key_points: Biphasic course: thyrotoxic (months 1 to 4) then hypothyroid (months 4 to 8)
Anti-TPO positive in approximately 70 to 80%
Distinguish from Graves: TRAb negative, reduced uptake on RAIU (low Doppler flow on USS as proxy in breastfeeding)
Treat thyrotoxic phase with beta-blocker; treat hypothyroid phase with thyroxine if symptomatic
20% develop permanent hypothyroidism; annual TFT screening thereafter
explanation.pearls: If TRAb is positive, think Graves disease (which can also present postpartum). If TRAb is negative and anti-TPO is high in a postpartum woman, think postpartum thyroiditis. Doppler ultrasound is a useful surrogate for radioiodine uptake in lactating women.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Thyroid Disease and Pregnancy 2017

subtopic_detail: Postpartum thyroiditis - biphasic course and management

stem: A 27-year-old G1P1 woman presents to the postnatal ward on day 5 following an elective lower segment caesarean section at 39+0 for breech presentation. She has had three temperatures recorded above 38 degC over the last 12 hours with associated rigors. Her caesarean wound is closed with absorbable sutures and on inspection appears clean, dry and intact without erythema, fluctuance or discharge. She is breastfeeding her infant from both breasts; both breasts feel soft, are not erythematous and have no localised tenderness or fluctuance. Her uterus is mildly tender on palpation in the lower abdomen and her lochia is heavier than expected and faintly offensive in odour. She has no flank pain, no dysuria, no frequency, and a urinalysis is negative for leucocytes and nitrites. Her calves are soft, non-tender and symmetrical with no swelling. She is not breathless and her SpO2 is 99% on room air. Bloods show WCC 16.4 with neutrophilia and CRP 124.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, T 38.6 degC, RR 20, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Caesarean wound clean, dry, intact. Breasts soft, no inflammation. Lower abdomen tender, uterus tender. Lochia heavy and faintly offensive. Calves soft. Renal angles non-tender.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.4 x10^9/L (neut 13.4), CRP 124 mg/L, urinalysis: leucs neg, nitrites neg. Blood cultures pending. HVS pending.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis given the bedside findings?

options.text: Caesarean wound infection with cellulitis and fever
options.rationale: Caesarean wound infection is wrong because the wound is intact with no erythema, fluctuance or discharge; wound infection would show cellulitis, induration, purulent discharge or seroma. Would be correct only with characteristic wound findings (RANZCOG).

options.text: Lactational mastitis with breast abscess and fever
options.rationale: Lactational mastitis is wrong because both breasts are soft, non-erythematous and non-tender; mastitis presents with hot, red, painful breast quadrant. Would be correct only with characteristic breast findings (ABA).

options.text: Urinary tract infection with pyelonephritis and fever
options.rationale: UTI/pyelonephritis is wrong because there is no dysuria, frequency or flank pain, urinalysis is negative for nitrites/leucocytes and renal angles are non-tender. Would be correct only with classic UTI features and positive urinalysis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Postpartum endometritis with secondary bacteraemia and fever
options.rationale: Fever on day 5 plus uterine tenderness plus heavy and faintly offensive lochia after caesarean section, with no breast, wound, urinary or respiratory focus, fits the classical triad of postpartum endometritis. Caesarean section is the leading risk factor and the polymicrobial source is the genital tract (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).

options.text: Deep vein thrombosis with low-grade thrombotic fever
options.rationale: DVT is wrong because calves are soft, non-tender and symmetrical; while postpartum DVT does occur, bedside findings localise to uterus and lochia rather than lower limb. Would be correct only with calf swelling and other DVT features (RCOG).
source_refs: RCOG GTG 37a Reducing the risk of VTE

explanation.summary: Postpartum fever differential follows the systematic '5 Ws plus Womb plus Wonder drugs': Wind (atelectasis/pneumonia), Water (UTI), Walking (DVT/PE), Wound (caesarean or perineum), Wonder drugs (drug reaction), Womb (endometritis), Whatever you forgot (mastitis, septic pelvic thrombophlebitis, line sepsis). The single best discriminator is the local focus on examination plus relevant investigations.
key_points: Endometritis: uterine tenderness + offensive lochia + caesarean section
Mastitis: focal breast erythema and tenderness, often unilateral wedge
Wound infection: erythema, discharge, fluctuance at incision
UTI: dysuria, frequency, positive urinalysis, flank tenderness
DVT/PE: calf tenderness/swelling, breathlessness, tachycardia disproportionate to fever
explanation.pearls: Postpartum endometritis is the single most common cause of postnatal fever between day 2 and day 10 and the most common puerperal infection after caesarean section. Always palpate the uterus and inspect the lochia explicitly.

sources.label: RCOG GTG 37a Reducing the risk of VTE

subtopic_detail: Postpartum fever differential - hooks for endometritis, wound infection, mastitis, UTI, DVT/PE
